So we're in the home stretch one more hour to go and now it's all about you and what you want and what your questions are. So I'll take a few from here but if you have questions, you know, raise your hand because I like, you know, when it's live. Structure versus flow. What is better in your experience when you've got a new idea? You know, I think when you first get a new idea, flow is better. You want the idea to flow. You want to put it down. You want to just write anything that comes into your head. Just keep writing, writing, writing, writing. Don't stop, don't stop, you know? There's even a program, it's called Write or Die and probably that probably sounds even better in German, right? You know, Richt or Tot. And it, when you, as long as you're typing, everything's okay. You stop typing, the program goes eh, eh, eh, like that. Oh, so loud, so annoying. Okay, I'll keep writing. You stop, it goes eh, eh, and it's very interesting though. I mean, it sounds horrible and it is. It sounds horrible. But it makes you just keep writing and you find that when you keep writing, you get a flow and it really helps. So I think get as much as you can down on paper and then have a structure, right? I'm going to talk in the beginning, right? Like we outlined, you know, for the headline, for the lead, needs to have these things, for the body, needs to have these things. The clothes, right? Offer, guarantee, scarcity, price, make it seem low, and then that, and then you've got your structure and you can put all that stuff that you've kind of flowed before into the structure. That's my answer to that one. Techniques for writing a strong headline, cliffhanger. Boy, that's a tough one. You know, I think the biggest secret is what we talked about, writing a hundred ideas. Another secret is to look at headlines that are famous, right? You can get lists of headlines and to take each of them and try to see how you can use that for your product, right? If the headline says, you know, what are famous headlines? Like they laughed when I sat down at the piano, but when I started to play, dot, dot, dot. Have you heard of that one? Yeah. Yeah, okay. So you can take that and look at that and say, oh, how can I use that? They laughed when I said I could predict any movement in the raw materials market, right? But when I started to actually do it, dot, dot, dot. So that's a very literal taking word for word. But then you could also not take it so literally. You could also take the concept behind it, right? Because there's a concept behind that. The concept behind it is that people ridiculing what you're going to be able to do and then you're being able to do it, right? That's the underlying structure. That's the underlying concept there. So you don't have to use those exact words. You could say, you know, nobody believed it when I said, you know, that I was going to be able to predict movements in the raw materials market or movements in the stock market, you know? Made my first million, dot, dot, dot, or when I bought copper before anyone else. You know what I mean? It doesn't have to use the exact same words, but it can trigger ideas. So that's what you want to do. You want to look at a list of headlines. You want to look at great ads and see what can that trigger. How many headlines ideas can I get from that? And then what we talked about before about the idea of doing the opposite. What can I do the opposite? I got a headline that says this. Okay, what's the opposite headline? I got a headline that says this. How can I add something to it? What if I take it away? What if I take away the idea of making money? What if I take away this little part of it? Is it stronger? Is it weaker? Is it different? How can I divide it up? Maybe I can take this one headline that I've tried to cram so much stuff into and divide it up into five headlines. What happens if I do that? Don't think about whether that's a good thing to do or not. Just do it and see what happens. The other trick is sort of one that we talked about, which is that fantasy idea. I think this is a Ted Nicholas thing. This idea of if I could say anything I wanted to, what would it be? And how he did that with the speech writing program. He said, what's the thing I would want more than anything? How to get a standing ovation every time you speak. And it worked as a headline. But maybe sometimes you think of something and you say, I can't say that. I can't say you will be as rich as Warren Buffett. So how close to that can I come? I could have the same returns as Warren Buffett. I could have a 19% return rate same as him. I could be as successful as him, maybe not over the lifetime because he's been successful for 30 years. But I could be as successful from now on. I could own the same stocks. How to invest like Warren Buffett? That's getting a little close to that. How to make money like Warren Buffett? So come up with that fantasy headline. See how close you can get to it. And another way to do it is if your advertising isn't strong enough, if you feel like it's not attention getting enough. Just come up with crazy ideas. Come up with crazy, come up with subject lines. People do this a lot. They'll think of something like banana ice cubes. And they'll use the word banana ice cubes in the subject line. You know, breakfast with banana ice cubes. And they'll have some wild way of talking about how somehow the banana got in the freezer and it became frozen and they used it for ice cubes. That reminds them of something and it connects to the product that they're selling. People like Ben Settle do that a lot. Just crazy subject lines to get your attention. Same thing with headlines. Just think of an interesting word related to investing. Think of a Zulu warrior when you're doing a product for men's health. How can I use that in the headline? How to have the potency of a Zulu warrior. The Zulu warrior secret for, you know. So sometimes you start with what gets attention. You don't try to take what you have and make it attention getting. You start with that explosion. You start with a body on the ground. I think we talked about that in the other group. One of the people, the safety people, their letter was about coming in and finding someone unconscious, right? And they weren't wearing a helmet and oh, that was a terrible thing and there was going to be trouble. But that's a great attention getter. Anytime you have a body on the floor, that's, you know, I couldn't believe the day I walked into my office and there was a body on the floor. Oh no, I'm the safety officer. You know, I'm responsible. You're trying to find the biggest, best one promise or idea for your copy. How do you evaluate your ideas? That's a really good question, you know. Because sometimes I'll forget to do certain things and I'll do something and it won't be as effective because I didn't go out and I didn't ask people whether they think it's effective. So the one way is to just show people, right? Can you look at this? Do you think would this get your attention? If you were interested in stocks, if you were a purchasing manager, if you were a human resource manager, if you were a tax guy, would this get your attention? And most people can say, oh yeah, if I was it would. It doesn't. There's nothing interesting in it. So that's where a lot of headlines comes in because you can keep showing it to people and saying, hey, what do you think about this? Nope, not yet. What do you think about this? Oh yeah, boy, that one would. And you also need to be able to feel. You need to, like Lori has been saying, and I've been saying, you need to become the reader, right? You need to be able to look at your copy and go, no, not doing it for me. And keep writing until you do to be able to almost talk to yourself. Say, David, what would you want to hear? I'd want to hear this. This would get my attention. So showing your idea to people. There's a lot of ways of testing on the internet now and Lori can tell you a lot about that because she's kind of working in the forefront of that. Ways of testing things online before you do a landing page, before you do direct mail, before you do things that are expensive, really cheap ways to test online. And then of course there's things like SurveyMonkey and very easy survey forms. There's ways of sending. You can send things to your customers. You can use subject lines, right? You can try something as a subject line. If the subject line doesn't get attention, doesn't get emails opened, it's maybe not going to work as a headline, that appeal. Does that help? Does that give you ideas of ways of testing, ways of finding out which of the promises is going to be the best one? How to tell a good story in seven sentences. One way is to read good stories that are only seven sentences, right? Just Google, world's shortest stories. Just Google stories of only a few sentences and you'll find those stories. Oh, Henry was an American writer who wrote a lot of stories. He wrote The Gift of the Magi, but he was famous for being able to tell a story in just a few words. Get the reader's full attention in less than 10 seconds with a short text. You know, all the things that get the reader's attention, the unexpected, the counterintuitive. You know what counterintuitive would be? You think one thing, but this is true, right? Get rich quick. You think you have to get rich quick. Then someone says get rich slowly, right? That gets someone's attention in a text because it's so unusual. You think that you have to, you think investing is hard work. I'm going to show you. Investing made easy. Investing for dummies. You know, that whole dummies book was if nothing else, it was a way of getting attention. It was like what? Dummies? Ooh, that's so unexpected to say that. But wait a minute. I do want something that's simple enough to be used by dummies. To be, you know, I like that. That's a nice, and then people bought millions of the books. So you want to do the unexpected. You want to do, make people see that they're wrong. Make people see that there's a huge opportunity, right, to make a lot of money. Make people see that there's a big disaster, something's coming that they need to know about. Make it clear to people they can be a hero. If you're just talking about less than 10 seconds in a text, it's got to be instantaneous. It's got to be had enough. It's got to be, what were we saying for someone? I can. Just very short ideas. They don't have to read it and go, oh, what does this mean? Let me figure it out. It's got to go right into their brains. My prospect has no time. How do I tell him or her the most important facts as shortly and quickly as possible? You know, you always want to be thinking that that's the case. You always want to be thinking your prospect has no time. You always want to be thinking they're in a rush, because that's how good copywriters write good copy, is we're always thinking, boy, this reader is just about to leave. I bore him for one second. He's going to be out the door. I've got to do everything I can to keep his attention. Hey, look over there. Hey, hey, hey. This is important. Hey, the world's going to blow up and you're going to get killed. Hey, people are making a lot of money. This could be you. You've got to have, here's a Zulu warrior. Look, look. You ever see a Zulu warrior before in an ad? No, isn't that incredible? So that's always how you have to be while you're writing. You can't ever get complacent, because people are, people have shorter and shorter attention spans. Every day, I think the attention span shrinks by 30 seconds. Pretty soon all we're going to have is 10 seconds to do things. What are your five must-haves for every copy? I think we talked a lot about different things. You've got to have the emotional component. You've got to have the drama. You've got to have the clarity, because if you don't have clarity, if you don't have simplicity, then you don't have anything else, because no one's going to read it. No one's going to work to read your copy. And the more you do the work, the more you connect the dots. Look, blah, blah, blah. That means this and this. It means all these things. Don't leave it to them to figure out what it means. Don't leave it to them to figure out why it's a good thing. Tell them exactly why he's making a million dollars a good thing. Because it'll enable you to go to Disneyland. It'll enable you to do the trips you want. It'll enable you to make your grandchildren have happy lives. It will enable you to drive the car of your dreams. It will make your wife or your husband so happy. Don't let them have to do the work of figuring out why it's a good thing. Don't let them have to do the work of figuring out why knowing prices of commodities in advance is a good thing, or why having certain tax information is a good thing, or why knowing how to work your computer better is a good thing. Show them that picture. Show them that movie. Which layout is suitable for a storytelling approach? I think I'll let Lori. For storytelling, if you're doing a landing page, is that what you're asking? Or more of a direct mail campaign? Don't do it, Gaggles. Yeah. All right, so for a direct mail campaign, sometimes maybe I won't try it first with a mag-a-log, which is like 16 pages. Maybe I will do some kind of an outer, and then maybe a little short, couple page letter, very personal. Then maybe like a little brochure style. Something different to tell the story and bring them in, depending on the product. You could do a mag-a-log and make it look very inspirational, helpful. Run that copy in a couple columns, make it look like a book. Sometimes for personal, I will just make it one column, and that seems to give that whole feeling of a story. I've done like an underlying story of how people needed electricity and light for solar power, PPX product, and all these trials and tribulations of their day, and how they could have used this charger, and then you show the charger. So that kind of a story and a certain kind of ending in the end sometimes is really fulfilling. Then for landing pages, I would just use the recipes or the mixes that I showed today, where you just let it run, you add the little photos in, some bullets, and break it up. But that story, like every single piece kind of has its own little story, and that's what tends to draw the person in towards you, close, or so you can talk to them. What about photos? Do you have photos that sort of illustrate the story as the story goes along, that follow along with it, or is that too obvious? I do, but I'm very careful on my photos. If you saw some of the photos that I showed today, that's at least an hour of looking for each photo, at least, and there's thousands of thousands of photos out there, as you know, by just searching in shutter stock or eye stock. So one trick that I do is sometimes if I key in couple running along the beach holding hands, I need it for a natural product, and it'll come up and it'll say there's 2,589 pages of photos. I won't even look at page one, two, or three. I will go to page 500. What's on page 500? Because most of the people competing with me are way lazier than I am, and they will use those photos. So I want something unique. And then an additional tip is, as you saw in some of the samples, we'll take a photo and sandwich it on top of another photo, so it's totally original and there's nothing else like that, the chalkboard in the background. Sometimes I'll even take a photo, and then in that photo I noticed that there's some fruits and berries or things. I will do photos on top of the photo that are three dimensional cutouts that may get interesting to look at. Anything that you can do to catch their eye. I love choosing photos. I think it's sort of like a lot like writing, you know, to be able to choose a photo that has something in it that's so unexpected. You're not expecting that photo, and yet at the same time it perfectly illustrates what you're trying to say. The Enquirer is great, not the Enquirer, the Economist has great photos. I don't know if you've ever seen the Economist, but they're not literal. It's not like what you're expecting. If they're talking about a meeting of the Federal Reserve Board, they're likely to show a picture of gorillas in the wild fighting with each other because there was some disagreement. All right. Well, I just want to say that the next step is for you guys to go back and to take what you've learned and really apply it. You know, a lot of times in seminars you learn things and, you know, it's like, oh, I'm excited, I'm motivated, and then a month later, after you've gotten all the work that's done waiting for you back at the office, you realize you haven't really applied it. But the way to really make these things deep in your brain and have them forever is to apply them tomorrow, right? To work on it tomorrow, to work on it Monday, to just keep writing, even if you just write a little bit every day, even if you just write a journal every day, you know, it keeps that muscle going. But just write a little bit of advertising every day. Come up with headlines every day. Come up with a little bit of body copy. Come up with a guarantee copy or copy for the order form, you know, and these things you'll just naturally apply them, you know, and show us what you're doing. You know, Laurie and I are interested to see how you take what we've done. So thank you for, you know, sitting patiently for four days. It's unbelievable four days that you sat here sitting patiently while we go all around the table and listening to people and listening to me up there and being so attentive and writing. You know, writing such good copy was so inspiring to see you guys come up with stuff. And to see how willing you were to do that. Thank you again. Thanks for being here and participating like this. Thank you. Thank you.
